skill.name: frontend-types
skill.description: Guides creation of TypeScript interfaces and type aliases for frontend API data, forms, query parameters, UI state, and errors.
skill.summary: Create consistent frontend TypeScript types that match backend API contracts.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution and weak-crypto hits, but review found these are false positives from Markdown backticks, TypeScript examples, and words such as description. No executable scripts, network calls, environment access, prompt injection text, or data exfiltration behavior were found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Commands
low_findings.description: The external command findings are false positives. The cited content is Markdown inline code and fenced TypeScript examples, not Ruby or shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines contain documentation and TypeScript snippets only. There are no shell commands, process execution APIs, or instructions to run external tools.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Crypto Pattern from Description Text
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings are false positives. The cited lines contain words like description or descriptive, plus TypeScript property examples, not cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Semantic review shows no hashing, encryption, random generation, or cryptographic API usage. The scanner appears to match text substrings inside ordinary documentation.

content.value_statement: Frontend teams need types that match backend API responses without mixing them with form data shapes. This skill provides repeatable patterns for interfaces, unions, generics, query parameters, errors, and UI state.
actual_capabilities: Defines interface patterns for users, tasks, API responses, authentication, errors, UI state, imports, and exports.
Shows when to use TypeScript interfaces versus type aliases.
Explains how API response fields can keep backend snake_case names.
Separates backend entity types from editable form data and query parameter types.
Demonstrates generic response wrappers and paginated response structures.
Documents naming conventions for entities, props, forms, and query parameters.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not modify files by itself.
It focuses on one frontend type file pattern, not every TypeScript architecture.
It assumes backend API contracts are already known.
It does not validate generated types against a live backend schema.

use_cases.title: Add Types for a New API Entity
use_cases.description: Create entity, form data, and query parameter types that follow the existing frontend conventions.

use_cases.title: Standardize API Response Contracts
use_cases.description: Use shared response and pagination types so API clients return predictable TypeScript shapes.

use_cases.title: Review Type Naming Consistency
use_cases.description: Check whether interfaces, unions, props, form data, and query parameter names follow the documented pattern.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Entity Types
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner task for adding one backend entity.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create frontend TypeScript types for a new Category entity using the frontend-types conventions. Include entity and form data types.

prompt_templates.title: Add Query Parameter Types
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate task for filterable API lists.
prompt_templates.prompt: Define query parameter types for a searchable and paginated task list. Follow the naming and optional-field patterns in frontend-types.

prompt_templates.title: Model API Responses
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate task for API client return values.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design API response and paginated response types for a frontend API client. Use generics and match the documented response pattern.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Existing Type Definitions
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the frontend TypeScript types for consistency with frontend-types. Identify naming issues, backend field mismatches, and missing form or query types.

output_examples.input: A developer needs types for a new Category API resource.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes an entity type that matches backend fields, a form data type for editable fields, and query parameters for search and pagination.

output_examples.input: A team wants consistent API client return types.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends a generic response wrapper, a paginated response shape, and entity-specific return types for each API method.

output_examples.input: A reviewer sees mixed snake_case and camelCase fields.
output_examples.output: The assistant separates backend response fields from frontend form fields and explains where conversion should occur.

best_practices: Keep API response types aligned with backend field names and required fields.
Use separate form data and query parameter types for user input and API filters.
Prefer descriptive PascalCase names for interfaces, unions, props, and response wrappers.
anti_patterns: Do not reuse backend entity types directly for editable form state.
Do not make every property optional when the backend always returns required fields.
Do not mix API response naming rules with frontend-only form naming rules.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guide with TypeScript examples and naming conventions.

faq.question: Where should the types be defined?
faq.answer: The skill points to a central frontend types file pattern, usually frontend/types/index.ts.

faq.question: Should API response fields use snake_case?
faq.answer: Yes, when the backend returns snake_case fields, response types should match that contract.

faq.question: Should form data use the same shape as API responses?
faq.answer: No. Form data should include only editable fields and can use frontend-friendly naming.

faq.question: Does it cover generic API wrappers?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes generic response and paginated response patterns.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
